summary: Paul Graham argues that many great startup ideas go unseen because founders have “schlep blindness” — an unconscious aversion to tedious, intimidating work. Startups require handling unpleasant tasks, and the scariest ideas are often the most valuable because others avoid them. He suggests overcoming this by recognizing that schleps are inevitable and by asking what problems you wish someone else would solve for you.
key_points: Schlep blindness is an unconscious tendency to avoid ideas that involve painful, tedious work.
Startups are defined by the schleps they take on; writing code alone is not enough.
Ambitious, scary ideas face less competition and can be undervalued opportunities.
Ignorance of the full scope of obstacles can help founders start and grow into the problems.
A useful prompt is to ask what problem you wish someone else would solve for you.
money_quotes: A company is defined by the schleps it will undertake.
And schleps should be dealt with the same way you'd deal with a cold swimming pool: just jump in.
That scariness makes ambitious ideas doubly valuable.
Instead of asking "what problem should I solve?" ask "what problem do I wish someone else would solve for me?"
key_insight: The best startup opportunities often hide behind intimidating, tedious work, and recognizing — then embracing — those schleps is a competitive advantage.
